speaker: interviewer
text: Anytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four, for Daniel Geslewitz. And you were just liberated. And you wanted to talk a little bit about the American troops.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were liberated May the 2nd, like I said before. And we were told not to leave on the grapevine. Not to leave the-- the camp, or we'll get-- help will be provided by the American army.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we decided-- my brother decided-- we had two more friends that we were close, from Lodz, two other brothers-- that we will not leave the camp. And we'll wait, see what's going to happen. But nothing happened. Nobody came to help us. So we stayed overnight. Next morning, nobody showed up either to help us. So we walked out of the camp. There was me, my brother, and the two other brothers. What was their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Baum. They live now in Israel. And we walked out. I could hardly walk. If I had to walk over obstacle, I had to lift up my foot, my hand, and put down on the other side, and walk around it. I was in very bad shape and had diarrhea. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we walked out towards Ludwilugst.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ludwilugst. L-U-D-W-I-L-U-G-S-T. Something like that. Ludwilugst.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we walked towards Ludwilugst. We came to Ludwilugst to search for food. We didn't have any food. I couldn't walk, so they left me in-- in front of a house, and it started to rain. And they went out to search for food. They came back, and they brought something, I don't know, cookie or something, this. But we had a place-- we-- we needed a place where to stay. So we walked into a German house. It was a one-family house. And at that time, Germans had another little house, where the-- for laundry-- a laundry room, something. So we walked in this laundry room, and we settled that laundry room. We didn't take over the big house because we-- we are not-- we-- we took the little house. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: The house was empty?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We found out people were there, but they hid in the-- in the attic because they were afraid of us. But we didn't walk in the big house. We took the little house. The-- the little room. One big room. That was the washroom, so to speak. And the first thing, we took off our clothes, and we started a fire. It was a big-- and we had to boil it, because we had to get rid of the lice. We had lice, lots of lice. And they were eating us alive. So we started the process, a process of you know, getting to normal to get rid of, you know, all those things that [INAUDIBLE] the lice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, and I couldn't walk, so I lie down on the floor. They put some blanket or something. And-- and then they send down the grandmother to find out what's going on. Because they wouldn't send down the daughter, because they're afraid we're going to rape her or something like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, we didn't-- that was the last thing on our mind, but they were afraid. The Germans were afraid. You could do-- at that time, you could do anything you want. You could take-- we could have taken over the house and kicked them out. But that was not in our nature, you know, to-- to rob. We didn't rob. After the war, we didn't rob.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she asked my brother, what's the matter with me, and my brother told her that I had diarrhea. So she came back with some-- she cooked up some soup or something, and I ate it. And as a matter of fact, this helped me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the-- as the food problem, so my brother and the other guy I used to go-- there were Americans, troops, stationed all over, and they had field kitchens, and they had lots of food. They had-- they had more than they needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they used to go to the kitchen, and they approached the chef, and they would give him leftovers. And that, for us, was a bonanza. It didn't matter what it was, as long as it was edible. So the food became less and less of a problem, because you made yourself familiar with a certain chef, and you used to go there, and they used to give you food. Cans. They gave us cans, and we didn't know what the cans-- what was inside, because you couldn't read English. But somehow, you-- you managed, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I-- I became the cook. Because they had to go out-- they could walk, my brother and the other two was-- and scrounge for food or clothing, and I couldn't walk, so I stayed in that room, and would cook whatever they bring home. I made noodles, they brought parts from chicken. Americans were getting fresh chickens, frozen chickens. But they didn't eat certain parts. But I-- from the parts, I would-- you-- you could cook soup. I'd cook chicken soup. So I became a chicken cook, you know, expert at making chicken soup. And so I would make big noodles, whatever. And so-- and I would eat and sleep. And I would wake up in the middle of the night and eat and go back to sleep. I was so far gone. But I was gaining strength, slowly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one day, the other three left this-- this little room, the house, and I was staying in the house by myself. A German-- an American MP barged in, into the room, with a German woman, or a girl. You know? She is the interpreter. And he-- you know, he-- he didn't know how to speak German, and I didn't know how to speak English, so she tells me in German I should leave the room and go outside. You know, and-- and go with them. And at that time, we respected authority. Everybody was giving us orders. So he-- he was very rude, and-- and, you know, like the MPs, real, real rude. Orders, you know? And at that time, I-- I wouldn't dare speak back to-- to-- to a soldier. So I left the room, went out in the street. There was a Jeep with a little-- how you call it? In in the back, that you-- how do you call it? That you-- the-- the soldiers had it. Oh, I sometimes think of a name, and I can take. You mean like a trailer?

speaker: interviewee
text: A trailer. You know, the Jeeps have trailers.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: With three guys sitting in the trailer, and they made me to sit in the trailer. You know? And they took off. I don't know where. I-- I didn't take anything with me. Just the way I was. No jacket, no nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He took us to a camp. Was a former German Air-- Air Force field, and-- and there was a guard, American guard, at the entrance. He drove through the gate.

speaker: interviewer
text: He made us get out, and they asked us what nationality? So if you happened to be Polish, he said go there. If you happened to be Yugoslavian, go there. And I happen to be Polish, you know, so you go there. Because Jewish, you know, being from Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here I'm walking around in that huge camp, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] could hardly, you know, still-- I'm still not well, could hardly walk. And I want to go home, without my brother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I walk around, and there were former prisoners, Polish prisoners of war sitting there in a group and talking.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I walk over, and I heard them speaking Polish, and I asked them, you know, Polish, how can you get out of this camp? And they tell me, over there's a gate, you can walk out of there. So I walked out of the camp. There were no-- the camp was not guarded at the perimeter, it was only at the gate.

speaker: interviewee
text: I walked out, and I walked to the field, and so far. I didn't know they had a curfew, but anybody-- and there were soldiers all over. And I was approached by another sentry, American sentry, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they marched me back into the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what the name of that camp was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was-- it was a former German airfield.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was not-- it was a temporary camp. That was like a transit camp. From there, they used to take all the foreigners that lived in Germany, Yugoslavs, them, they used to ship them back into their respective countries.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I did-- they didn't ask me whether I want to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here I am in this-- in this position. I didn't realize. Finally, I walked out a third time, out of the camp. And I'm walking, and I was caught again, but there were a group of people that we're together in concentration camp. You know, from Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as you walk in, there was one guy who could speak English, from us. And he said, you know what? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He understood. He said, when we get to the camp, we're going to say that we're German Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we're German Jews, where are they going to send us? We're in Germany. So they walked us-- as we walked, you know, like, we didn't walk in a group. Because you could hardly walk. One walked here, like, how you call it, in a-- in a line, stretched out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And an American soldier, he got very impatient with me because I couldn't walk fast. A whole day walking back and forth. So he pulled out a pistol, and he commanded me something in-- in English, and I don't know what he's talking about. And I cannot walk any faster.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he fires in front of-- of my-- of my stomach. He shoot-- he fired to-- to scare me. But he didn't scare me, because after all we went through, you know, it didn't scare me, and I kept on walking. I-- I couldn't walk any faster. So we came to the third time, the fourth-- to the same gate. And they said, you know what nationality? And he said, German Jews, and he didn't know how-- he said, don't talk Polish. Don't talk anything. Just-- just German.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- and we could talk German good. We said, German Jews. He said, you can't come in here. That's only for foreigners, not for German Jews. You better-- you-- you cannot come in this camp. So, fine. That was fine with us. You walked back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as you walked back, we walked back into the city, so we knew now the-- the-- the the secret word. German Jews, you know? So as we were-- as we were passing sentries in the way, we said, we're German Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got to the city, there was an officer, and he stopped us. An American officer. And he asked us, and we said we're German Jews. He said, yes. There's a curfew, he said, till 5 o'clock. You walk in-- you go into that backyard, he said. You stay there till 5 o'clock. 5 o'clock, you're free to go. So we went to the backyard, and there were other people-- we couldn't-- nobody could walk in the city. And after 5 o'clock, we could go back home. But I didn't know where my home is. I-- I-- I didn't-- I never went out of this-- out of this room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't know where Leon was, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know where Leon was. I didn't know what happened to him. And I didn't know where our home is, where-- where I'm staying. But I knew our house was not far from the railroad station. So I asked people, the Germans, where is the Bahnhoff, you know, in German. So they directed me. And being in this particular by the Bahn-- I found the house. Then he came back, and the other two brothers. He wasn't taken, because he went to work in the morning-- he worked in the kitchen the whole day, and he wasn't exposed to that. They would go from house to house taking out all the foreigners and sending them back to their respective countries.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went through this ordeal, this bad-- this. But so happened, I did not go back to Poland. I had-- for some reason, I had sense enough, you know, to-- to get out of there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I-- I-- I knew I had nobody in Poland, and the only place that was alive was my brother, and I didn't want to be separated from him. So this was a bad experience. I didn't realize at that time, when this was happening, what bad experience it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did the two of you do after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, we-- all four, the other two brothers--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we couldn't go anyplace because of our health reasons. We were not strong enough to travel. And the only place we think to go was-- think to go was back to Poland, because this was our home. You know? Even-- we-- we knew everybody's gone, but this is still Poland, you're born in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after six weeks or seven weeks, we decided now we're ready to go back to Poland. All together, all four. So we went to the same place as they took me, but we went there voluntarily. We had already accumulated some food, and something, you know. And the backpacks, we packed everything in.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to this airfield. And at that time, there were no more American. There were English. Because this part was given back to the Russians. It became the Eastsaw, the-- the-- you know, the-- the Russian occupation zone. So we went to the same airfield, and there were English soldiers there. And so they put us on a-- on a truck. Instead of shipping us east, there were no transport going to the east. In other words, the border was closed. And they couldn't send us to the east, so they send us west. And west-- and-- and they send us west, you know. We crossed the Elba, but they provide-- the American provide transportation, and-- and we landed in Lynnebrook. We didn't know where we'd take us to Lynnebrook.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it happened, they took us to Lynnebrook, to an English army camp, where English soldiers were stationed. And they quartered us in this camp. And this camp was a good camp. Was this a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was an American army camp. But they had part, a transient camp. In other words, they had--

speaker: interviewer
text: For people who were immigrating.

speaker: interviewee
text: For people that were displaced, they would keep them there until transport was available. They would send them-- we had French-- former French prisoners of war, the Italians, and you had there from all over, prisoners of war. And they kept them there till transport was available. You send them back to their countries. And we were landed in this camp. And this camp was a good camp, because the food was excellent. The English behaved very nice towards us. We came and-- to this camp, and the soldiers helped us take off the luggage, you know, and-- and I watched the luggage. And those three went into a-- they were ordered to go into a building, you know, the-- the German army camps, they had those red buildings. And I was watching the luggage, whatever we had, you know. The-- they came out with cots. You know, I don't know what the cots were for at that particular time. I suppose-- and-- and then they took the luggage and said we should into the building. As we walked in the building, there was an English soldier handing out cards. You know, a card. And he walked up-- they made us go up on the second floor, the third. And every time, on each floor was a soldier who's supposed to be-- you're going to be quartered there, handing out cards. By the time we wound up on the fourth floor, we had four cards. These four cards were a bonanza, because they're food cards. In other words, you-- it was a mess hall.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you've got, now, cards for four people.

speaker: interviewee
text: For four people. And for four-- if they gave us cigarettes, I got for four people cigarettes. And those cards were a bonanza, because I could eat four breakfasts, four lunches, four suppers. So I used to save the bread and eat at night, wake up in the middle of the night and eat a loaf of bread. Like, the bread was white. Beautiful, you know, and the food was good. You know, the same English. We had breakfast, you know, and then we had lunch, and tea time, and crumpets, and all those things. They-- they fed us good. There where I regained strength. And there where we met Jewish girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aha.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not girls like-- not what you have in mind. We walked in the street, and we met three girls. They were three Jewish German girls. And they lived on German [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] food rations, because they were German citizens. They could--

speaker: interviewer
text: So we gave them cards. We had three cards. We gave our cards, and then we took them to camp. We introduced them, that they are cousins. It was a-- it was a-- you know, you look at that like you [INAUDIBLE]. That was very important. To them, was food. And we supplied them with the cards, and they used to come and eat in the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, you could walk in and walk out any time you want. So they came for lunch, or for the day, and we gave them the cards, and it was a big thing. You think like this, you go into the supermarket, and you buy whatever you want. In those days, you couldn't buy it. There was not-- it wasn't there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we established-- slowly, we established a community, a Jewish community. We took it-- there were 17 Hungarian girls in the hospital, and we took them out, too. Each one found a cousin, and we went to the-- to the English officer, and he introduces another cousin. But the German, as we were walking for food, I-- we would walk in, get the ration, you know, whatever they put on your plate, eat, walk out, and walk around, and walk again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the-- the-- there were German girls who worked there. They were serving. And they got wise to us. And they say, you just walked by. So one of us went to the German-- to the English commanding officer and had a talk with him. I, to the guy, said, listen. We walked two times through, but we-- we suffered so much during the war. And she didn't want to give us any food. She refused. So the-- the English officer called together all the personnel, and they told them, any time they walk by, you give them food. It was very nice of them. And because we were used to-- you know, to all-- and here, we show the concern about one human being to the other human being, and it was very nice of him. And so we-- we got all stragglers, you know, from all over, and we got together, and we formed a Jewish community in the city of [NON-ENGLISH]. And we had our own restaurant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We formed a restaurant. We had services. We had dances. Only for the-- for-- we had Jews from all over. We had Jews-- we had Polish Jews, Hungarian Jewish, Romanian Jews. From Greece, we had Jews. And then, the English Jewish soldiers used to patronize us. They used to come and spend time with us every evening. That was nice, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And is this where you met your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And-- and I met my wife in-- in June in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1948. Yeah, but everything was coincidence. You know, the-- the boys, most of them, the-- the refugees, you know, the survivors married survivors. We didn't marry-- go out with German girls. There were plenty of German girls for food, you know, at that time. We didn't associate with them. We-- we had our-- we-- we had-- we are stuck together by fate. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you actually-- you knew your wife for two years before you got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, I knew my wife for two years. And then we got married in 1948. Because we-- we-- we-- the Jewish, the survivors, the stragglers-- we sort of-- we had-- like we felt like family. Not like-- we felt a kinship to each other because we went through all those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we supported each other. And boy meets girl, and I know-- or they-- they-- then we had weddings. All the girls were married in the same dress, wedding dress, you know? And who married you?

speaker: interviewee
text: A rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a rabbi there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there was a rabbi. He came from Belsen, from-- we were in [NON-ENGLISH], but, you know, the famous Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was about 40 kilometers away, about 30 miles. So he came, and he married us. We had a strictly Jewish wedding. I have pictures of the wedding. That was really good, in those days, you know, were good. We already established ourself, we-- we-- we-- you know, we-- we felt like human beings again. And so, slowly, it's a slow process of-- of getting back on your feet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And when did you immigrate to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1950. In 1950, I came to the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: To New York. My wife was pregnant. She was seven months pregnant. And they're all-- they took-- the-- after we were processed, you know, it's not like you-- after you're processed, we had to pass an-- you know, a medical test. All those tests, we had to pass it. After that, she was pregnant seven months, so they took all the pregnant women, something, and put on the plane with the husband, and we flew to New York. And when we came to New York, they took us by bus to-- to-- to the Penn Station. And from there, they were sending away people to all over the United States. There were Christian organizations, you know, all kind of-- and they used to ship them to Kansas, or whatever. Put them on the trains. But we-- our destination was New York. And when we got there, I wrote to my family, whatever, you know, I never knew them, that I'm coming. When I got to Penn Station, we were sitting there, both of us, and nobody's there to-- to say hello to us. And we see to those people somebody's coming, and-- and-- you know, taking care of those-- this couple. And us, nobody is there. Finally, a man came, and he looked around, he spotted us, and he walked over. He was German. He asked us whether we were Mr. and Mrs. Geslewitz. And I said yes. And he said to us, your family [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] choose not to come and take you, and take care of you. So we should come, go with him, or come with him. And we went with him. We didn't have a choice. We'd have gone with anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he took us to the HIAS, you know, on Lafayette Street. And it was terrible. We didn't sleep, and so. We went to there, and it was-- she suffered a lot, my wife. I-- I could've taken it, but she was pregnant, and there was so noisy place, they were terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had a cousin, and I figured, you don't come to me, I'll go to you. There's no pride. No-- you know, necessity. We took a taxi, and the taxi ask me whether-- he found out, through the telephone book. He said give me a name, I'll find out where he lives. He spoke Yiddish. You know, you had plenty of Jewish people in New York that could speak Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he tells me, he said, I found it. She lives in the Bronx. He said, but it will cost you $4. Do you have $4? I said, I'll give you $4. He took us to the cousin that we-- this is the only cousin I knew from Lodz. No, two. But we went there, and we surprised them.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there one night. Next night, she put us on the subway. She sent us back to the HIAS. Came back to HIAS. They knew we weren't there, so we explained to them. So they put my wife in one room for other women, and I slept in the attic, you know, with the men. And-- and slowly, you know, they took care of us. They tried their best to take care of us. And-- and they sent me to Park Row. I guess it was 15 Park Row in New York, where they took care on all the-- the-- the HIAS took care of all the-- they joined to care-- and the needs of the refugees.

speaker: interviewer
text: I got there, and there was a-- a social worker. She was nice. She tried to help, and she asked me what I need. What do I need? I haven't got any place to sleep. I need. She said you need pots, you need pans, you need blankets, you need this. I said, I need it. I said, work, I said, I'll find work. I said, three weeks will go by, I'll look for work, and I'll find work. She said, people here five months, they find work, and you just got here, she said you're going to work for three weeks? Listen, I didn't argue with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I called up a friend that he lived in the same Germany with us. And he left for America before, for the States. So I called him up, and he recommended somebody. I should go, he told me somebody, and I-- within three weeks, I had a job. And but-- and I went to the HIAS, and they sent me-- they told me, if I find a job in my profession, they'll give me all the tools I need. So I went, they sent me to a place in Brooklyn, and I picked up a bunch of tools.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you did find a job in your profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but that didn't work. I didn't need the tools. They made me work, you know, not carrying lumber. But I had to pay the price. They made me carry lumber from the-- to the third floor. It was hard, but I didn't have a choice. I figured, you know, I'm here, got to play the game, I hope. And I worked, and then I worked for a company that were making window frames, you know, the big window frames. And I worked with-- with another Italian guy. He worked on one side of the bench, and I worked on the other side of the bench. He was getting paid $2.25, and I was getting paid $0.75. You know, everybody took advantage.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I figured, I got to-- I have to pay the price for it. I had no choice. Slowly, slowly, but it paid off, because slowly, slowly, I-- I worked myself up, and I learned, you know, the way of life here in the-- in-- when-- you know, slow, it went better and better. But it was not easy by a long shot. You know? So your first baby was born in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first baby was born in New York. In-- in the New York Infirmary, the women's infirmary. And they didn't let me go up to see the baby. You know, it was-- it was very hard. You know, nobody-- by yourself, you don't know the language. You don't know, really-- and my family, it's comical.

speaker: interviewer
text: My family told me, we lived-- we had the-- we had an apartment building. And they said, when your baby, when she's going to get ready, call the police. What do I know? I called the police. Two guys came, two policemen, three. And the guy told me, it was no emergency. So he realized, that I'm a greenhorn, and he said you know what? I'll call a taxi. And the hospital supposed to go is-- is New York. We lived in Brooklyn, to go to New York. So he said, I'll give you-- I'll get you a taxi. But you look at the meter, and you pay what's on the meter. He tried to educate me. So the guy took me to Brooklyn-- to New York, and I left my wife in the infirmary. And I went home, because they didn't let me stay there. And with the taxi, and the next day I went to work, next day. Or two days later, I guess. Two days later-- no, our youngest was born. Oh, good. We have to stop this tape, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Yeah.
